title: CBP Workload Staffing Model Act
plain_summary: This bill tells U.S. Customs and Border Protection to create plans that show how many workers they need and where to put them. They have to make these plans for the Border Patrol agents who work at the border and for the people who fly planes and boats to protect the country. The agency also has to build computer systems to keep track of their workers and report to Congress about their progress. Finally, government inspectors will check if the new plans follow the advice they gave before.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Border Security and Enforcement.
subjects: Government employee pay, benefits, personnel management

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the CBP Workload Staffing Model Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Establishment of Workload Staffing Models for U.S. Border Patrol and Air and Marine Operations of U.S. Customs and Border Protection
sections.text: The head of U.S. Customs and Border Protection (the agency that guards our borders and ports) must work with other Department of Homeland Security officials to create staffing models. A staffing model is a plan that shows how many workers are needed and where they should be placed to do the job well. The Commissioner must create these models within one year after this law is passed. The models must be made for two parts of the agency: the U.S. Border Patrol (agents who patrol the border between the United States and other countries) and Air and Marine Operations (agents who use boats and aircraft to protect borders and waterways). The existing Homeland Security Act of two thousand and two is being changed to add new duties for the Commissioner. The Commissioner must create a staffing model that looks at important frontline work activities, different working environments, current and future buildings and equipment, current and future technology, and the support staff needed for the U.S. Border Patrol, Air and Marine Operations, and the Office of Field Operations (the part that manages ports of entry). This model helps make sure each location has enough people and resources to do their jobs. The Commissioner must also create standard procedures for a system that tracks the workforce in these three divisions. All workers in these divisions must be trained on how to use this tracking system and understand what it does. The Commissioner must also put in place controls to make sure work hours and activities are scheduled and reported correctly and on time. Within one year after this law passes, the Secretary of Homeland Security must send a report to two congressional committees: the House Committee on Homeland Security and the Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs. This first report must explain how well the new staffing models and workforce tracking requirements are being put into place. After the first report, the Secretary must send similar reports every year. These reports must include information about how the staffing models work and what data and methods were used to create them. Within one hundred and twenty days after the Commissioner creates the workload staffing models, the Inspector General of the Department of Homeland Security (an independent office that reviews government agencies) must review these models. The Inspector General must give feedback to the Secretary of Homeland Security and the same two congressional committees. This feedback should explain how well the new models follow previous Inspector General recommendations, including recommendations from a February two thousand and nineteen audit. The Inspector General can also suggest additional improvements to make the models work better.
